key_concepts: permanent tissues (cardiac muscle, skeletal muscle, nerve)
hypoxia as cause of cellular injury
covered: permanent tissues (cardiac muscle, skeletal muscle, nerve)
missing: hypoxia as cause of cellular injury

judge_scores.justification: Tests two related concepts (homeostasis and growth adaptations) rather than being perfectly atomic.
Tests the definition and mechanism together, slightly reducing atomicity.
Tests both the definition of hyperplasia and the source of new cells in one card.
Perfect card testing a single fact about tissue types and their growth limitations.
Requires understanding what 'permanent tissues' means from external context.
Excellently atomic card testing a single clinical exception.
Clear, atomic card about hyperplasia progression.
Tests three facts: definition of atrophy, and changes in both cell size and number.
Perfect atomic card about a single mechanism of atrophy.
Tests two mechanisms (ubiquitin-proteosome and autophagy) in one answer.
Tests both the definition and the most common location in one card.
Tests three facts: tissue types involved and the causative factor.
Tests both reversibility and the condition for reversal.
Clean, atomic card about metaplasia progression.
Tests both the definition and its association with precancerous cells.
Tests two causes (hyperplasia and metaplasia) rather than one.
Perfect atomic card about dysplasia irreversibility.
Tests definitions of two different conditions in one card.
Tests both what CIN represents and its relationship to cancer.
Clean, atomic card about dysplasia reversibility.
Perfect definition card for aplasia.
Clear, atomic definition of hypoplasia.
Excellent atomic card defining cellular injury.
Perfect example of an atomic, clear medical fact.
Another excellent atomic card about ischemic resistance.
Perfect card - tests single fact about hypoxia's mechanism clearly and accurately.
Excellent basic definition card with no weaknesses.
Tests two facts: definition of hypoxemia AND the specific PaO2 cutoff value.
Tests multiple facts: normal PaO2, decreased O2 sat, AND CO's binding mechanism.
While accurate, cherry-red skin is less clinically essential than other CO poisoning facts.
Tests two facts: oxidation state of iron AND its functional consequence.
Tests two clinical findings but they're closely related manifestations.
Oversimplified - reversible injury includes more than just cellular swelling.
Correctly identifies the key feature distinguishing irreversible injury.
Perfect card linking specific pump failure to its consequence.
Clear and atomic card about calcium pump dysfunction.
Essential clinical concept explained clearly and atomically.
Perfect card about specific apoptosis mechanism.
Tests three separate nuclear changes and their definitions in one card.
Appropriately asks for the list of three related changes as a single concept.
Tests two characteristics of necrosis: large groups AND inflammation.
Tests three facts: ATP-dependence, single cells, AND no inflammation.
Slightly non-atomic by including comparison to necrosis in the answer.
Tests four separate characteristics of coagulative necrosis in one card.
Perfect card linking necrosis type to clinical scenario.
Tests both appearance and mechanism, though closely related.
Appropriately groups related organs showing same necrosis type.
Tests two descriptors of appearance, though closely related.
Perfect high-yield association card.
Tests appearance, calcium deposition, AND saponification mechanism in one card.
Perfect card testing a single, clear medical fact about fat necrosis locations.
Excellent card testing one specific fact about dystrophic calcification.
Tests two separate facts (location and appearance) in one card, reducing atomicity.
Clear and atomic card about conditions showing fibrinoid necrosis.
Tests two distinct caspase functions in one card, compromising atomicity.
Perfectly atomic card about a specific consequence of Bcl2 inactivation.
Tests two triggers in one answer, slightly reducing atomicity.
Clear, atomic card testing recognition of apoptosis characteristics.
Perfectly focused on one specific protease function.
Perfectly focused on one specific endonuclease function.
Tests two related but distinct facts (Bcl2 inactivation and cytochrome c release).
Tests both ligand and receptor names in one card, slightly reducing atomicity.
Tests two distinct mechanisms (perforins and granzymes) in one answer.
Tests three facts: definition, lipid peroxidation, and DNA/protein oxidation.
Tests enzyme name, substrate, and product in one card.
Excellent atomic card about reperfusion injury mechanism.
Tests both structural configuration and staining property in one card.
Perfect card testing one specific histological finding.
Clear, atomic card linking AL amyloid to plasma cell disorders.
Clear, atomic card linking AA amyloid to chronic inflammation.
Tests three separate clinical manifestations in one card, severely compromising atomicity.
